name: CHEMISTRY (Theory)
question_paper_html:
Annual Exam - 2025

Code: 56/7/2 (set-2)

  1. Please check that this question paper contains 23 printed pages.
  2. Please check that this question paper contains 33 questions.
  3. Q.P. Code given on the right hand side of the question paper should be written on the title page of the answer-book by the candidate.
  4. Please write down the serial number of the question in the answer-book at the given place before attempting it.
  5. 15 minute time has been allotted to read this question paper. The question paper will be distributed at 10.15 a.m. From 10.15 a.m. to 10.30 a.m., the candidates will read the question paper only and will not write any answer on the answer-book during this period.

CHEMISTRY (Theory)

Time allowed:3 hours
Maximum Marks:70

General Instructions :

Read the following instructions carefully and follow them:

  1. This question paper contains 33 questions. All questions are compulsory.
  2. This question paper is divided into five sections - Section A, B, C, D and E.
  3. Section A - questions number 1 to 16 are multiple choice type questions. Each question carries 1 mark.
  4. Section B - questions number 17 to 21 are very short answer type questions. Each question carries 2 marks.
  5. Section C - questions number 22 to 28 are short answer type questions. Each question carries 3 marks.
  6. Section D - questions number 29 and 30 are case-based questions. Each question carries 4 marks.
  7. Section E - questions number 31 to 33 are long answer type questions. Each question carries 5 marks.
  8. There is no overall choice given in the question paper. However, an internal choice has been provided in few questions in all the sections except Section -A.
  9. Kindly note that there is a separate question paper for Visually Impaired candidates.
  10. Use of calculator is NOT allowed.

Section - A

Questions no. 1 to 16 are Multiple Choice type Questions, carrying 1 mark each. (16 {tex}\times{/tex} 1 = 16)
1.

Among the following outermost configurations of transition metals which one shows the highest oxidation state?

a. 3d34s2
b. 3d54s1
c. 3d54s2
d. 3d64s2
1
2.

Which of the following solution in water will have the lowest freezing point?

a. {tex}1 \% {KCl}{/tex}
b. {tex}1 \%{/tex} glucose
c. {tex}1 \%{/tex} urea
d. {tex}1 \% {CaCl}_{2}{/tex}
1
3.

Kohlrausch gave the following relation for strong electrolytes:
{tex} \Lambda_{\mathrm{m}}=\Lambda_{\mathrm{m}}^{\circ}-\mathrm{A} \sqrt{\mathrm{C}} {/tex}
Which of the following equality holds true?

a. {tex}\wedge_{\mathrm{m}}=\wedge_{\mathrm{m}}^{\circ}{/tex} as {tex}\mathrm{C} \longrightarrow 1{/tex}
b. {tex}\wedge_{\mathrm{m}}=\wedge_{\mathrm{m}}^{\circ}{/tex} as {tex}\mathrm{C} \longrightarrow 0{/tex}
c. {tex}\wedge_{\mathrm{m}}=\wedge_{\mathrm{m}}^{\circ}{/tex} as {tex}\mathrm{C} \longrightarrow \infty{/tex}
d. {tex}\wedge_{\mathrm{m}}=\wedge_{\mathrm{m}}^{\circ}{/tex} as {tex}\mathrm{C} \longrightarrow \sqrt{\mathrm{A}}{/tex}
1
4.

Unit of rate constant 'k' for a second order reaction is:

a. {tex}{s}^{-1}{/tex}
b. {tex}{mol} {L}^{-1} {~s}^{-1}{/tex}
c. {tex}{mol}^{-1} {~L} {~s}^{-1}{/tex}
d. {tex}{mol}^{-2} {~L} {~s}^{-1}{/tex}
1
5.

Which of the following represents the fraction of molecules with energies equal to or greater than {tex}{E}_{{a}}{/tex}?

a. {tex}\frac{-{E}_{{a}}}{{RT}}{/tex}
b. {tex}e^{-E_{a} / R T}{/tex}
c. {tex}e^{+{E}_{a} / {RT}}{/tex}
d. {tex}+\frac{{E}_{{a}}}{{RT}}{/tex}
1
6.

The number of moles of AgCl precipitated when excess {tex}{AgNO}_{3}{/tex} solution is mixed with one mole of {tex}\left[{Co}\left({NH}_{3}\right)_{4} {Cl}_{2}\right] {Cl}{/tex} is:

a. 1
b. 0
c. 2
d. 3
1
7.

The synthesis of alkyl fluoride is best obtained from:

a. Free radicals
b. Swartz reaction
c. Sandmeyer reaction
d. Finkelstein reaction
1
8.

In the reaction {tex}\mathrm{R}-\mathrm{OH}+\mathrm{HCl} \stackrel{\mathrm{ZnCl}_2}{\longrightarrow} \mathrm{RCl}+\mathrm{H}_2 \mathrm{O}{/tex}, what is the correct order of reactivity of alcohol?

a. 1o < 2o < 3o
b. 1o > 3o > 2o
c. 1o > 2o > 3o
d. 3o > 1o > 2o
1
9.

Phenol reacts with Bromine {tex}\left({Br}_{2}\right){/tex} water to form:

a. 2, 4-dibromophenol
b. 2-bromophenol
c. 2, 4, 6-tribromophenol
d. 4-bromophenol
1
10.

When excess of alkyl iodide is treated with ammonia, the product obtained is:

a. Primary amine
b. Secondary amine
c. Tertiary amine
d. A mixture of amines
1
11.

An amine 'X' reacts with Hinsberg reagent and the product obtained is insoluble in alkali. The amine 'X' is:

a. {tex}\left(\mathrm{CH}_3\right)_2-\mathrm{NH}{/tex}
b. {tex}\left(\mathrm{CH}_3\right)_3 \mathrm{~N}{/tex}
c. {tex}\mathrm{CH}_3-\mathrm{CH}_2-\mathrm{NH}_2{/tex}
d.
1
12.

'Scurvy' is caused due to the deficiency of:

a. Vitamin A
b. Vitamin B2
c. Vitamin C
d. Vitamin D
1

For Questions number 13 to 16, two statements are given - one labelled as Assertion (A) and the other labelled as Reason (R). Select the correct answer to these questions from the codes (A), (B), (C) and (D) as given below.

  1. Both Assertion (A) and Reason (R) are true and Reason (R) is the correct explanation of the Assertion (A).
  2. Both Assertion (A) and Reason (R) are true, but Reason (R) is not the correct explanation of the Assertion (A).
  3. Assertion (A) is true, but Reason (R) is false.
  4. Assertion (A) is false, but Reason (R) is true.
13.

Assertion (A): A mixture of o-nitrophenol and p-nitrophenol can be separated by steam distillation.
Reason (R): o-nitrophenol is steam volatile due to intermolecular hydrogen bonding.

a. Both A and R are true and R is the correct explanation of A.
b. Both A and R are true but R is not the correct explanation of A.
c. A is true but R is false.
d. A is false but R is true.
1
14.

Assertion (A): Cooking time is reduced in pressure cooker.
Reason (R): Boiling point of water inside the pressure cooker is elevated.

a. Both A and R are true and R is the correct explanation of A.
b. Both A and R are true but R is not the correct explanation of A.
c. A is true but R is false.
d. A is false but R is true.
1
15.

Assertion (A): Actinoids show irregularities in their electronic configurations.
Reason {tex}(R){/tex}: This is due to varying stability of {tex}{f}^{0}, {f}^{7}{/tex} and {tex}{f}^{14}{/tex} configurations.

a. Both A and R are true and R is the correct explanation of A.
b. Both A and R are true but R is not the correct explanation of A.
c. A is true but R is false.
d. A is false but R is true.
1
16.

Assertion (A): Maltose is a reducing sugar.
Reason {tex}(R){/tex}: One of the two glucose units can open to expose free aldehydic group in solution.

a. Both A and R are true and R is the correct explanation of A.
b. Both A and R are true but R is not the correct explanation of A.
c. A is true but R is false.
d. A is false but R is true.
1

Section - B
17.

What is meant by positive deviation from Raoult's law? Give an example. What type of azeotrope is formed by positive deviation?

 2
18.(i)

Write the IUPAC name of the complex {tex}\left[{Pt}({en})_{2} {Cl}_{2}\right]^{2+}{/tex}. Draw the structure of geometrical isomer of this complex which is optically inactive.

 2

OR
(ii)
  1. Write the formula of the following coordination compound: Pentaamminecarbonatocobalt (III) chloride
  2. Write the IUPAC name of the linkage isomer of the complex {tex}\left[{Co}\left({NH}_{3}\right)_{5}\left({NO}_{2}\right)\right] {Cl}_{2}{/tex}.
 2
19.

State a condition under which a bimolecular reaction is kinetically first order reaction. Give an example. For which type of reactions, do order and molecularity have the same value?

 2
20.

Write two differences between Amylose and Amylopectin components of starch.

 2
21.

Why are haloarenes less reactive towards nucleophilic substitution reaction? How does the presence of nitro ({tex}-{NO}_{2}{/tex}) group at ortho-and para-positions in haloarenes increase the reactivity towards nucleophilic substitution reaction?

 2

Section - C
22.

Differentiate between:

  1. Essential amino acids and Non-essential amino acids
  2. Peptide linkage and Glycosidic linkage
  3. Fibrous protein and Globular protein
 3
23.

Give reasons for the following:

  1. Benzoic acid does not undergo Friedel-Crafts reaction.
  2. {tex}{HCHO}{/tex} is more reactive than {tex}{CH}_{3} {CHO}{/tex} towards addition of HCN.
  3. Vinyl group directly attached with carboxylic acid should decrease the acidity of corresponding carboxylic acid due to resonance, but on the contrary it increases the acidity.
 3
24.

Write structure of the products of the following reactions:

 3
25.

The following data were obtained during the first order thermal decomposition of {tex}{N}_{2} {O}_{5}({~g}){/tex} at constant volume:
{tex}2 {~N}_{2} {O}_{5}({~g}) \longrightarrow 2 {~N}_{2} {O}_{4}({~g})+{O}_{2}({~g}){/tex}
Calculate rate constant.

S. No. Time/s Total Pressure/atm
1 0 0.5
2 100 0.625

[Given: {tex}\log 2=0 \cdot 3010, \log 10=1{/tex}]

 3
26.

A compound (A) with molecular formula {tex}{C}_{4} {H}_{9} {I}{/tex} which is a primary alkyl halide, reacts with alcoholic KOH to give compound (B). Compound (B) reacts with HI to give (C) which is an isomer of (A). When (A) reacts with Na metal in the presence of dry ether, it gives a compound (D), {tex}{C}_{8} {H}_{18}{/tex}, which is different from the compound formed when {tex}n{/tex}-butyl iodide reacts with sodium. Write the structures of (A), (B), (C) and (D). Write the chemical equation when compound (A) is reacted with alcoholic KOH.

 3
27.(i)

Write the name of the cell which is generally used in inverters. Write the reactions taking place at anode and cathode of this cell, when it is in use.

 3

OR
(ii)

Explain why electrolysis of aqueous solution of NaCl gives H2 at cathode and Cl2 at anode. Write overall reaction.
{tex}\left( \begin{gathered} E_{N{a^ + }/Na}^0 = - 2.71V;\;E_{{H_2}O/{H_2}}^0 = - 0.83V \hfill \\ E_{C{l_2}/2C{l^ - }}^0 = + 1.36V;\;E_{{H^ + } + {O_2}/{H_2}O}^0 = 1.23V \hfill \\ \end{gathered} \right){/tex}

 3
28.

{tex}0 \cdot 3 {~g}{/tex} of acetic acid (Molar mass {tex}=60 {~g} {~mol}^{-1}{/tex}) dissolved in 30 g of benzene shows a depression in freezing point equal to {tex}0.45^{\circ} {C}{/tex}. Calculate the percentage association of acid if it forms a dimer in the solution. (Given: {tex}{K}_{{f}}{/tex} for benzene {tex}=5 \cdot 12 {~K} {~kg} {~mol}^{-1}{/tex})

 3

Section - D

The following questions are case-based questions. Read the case carefully and answer the questions that follow.
29.

Amines are usually formed from amides, imides, halides, nitro compounds, etc. They exhibit hydrogen bonding which influences their physical properties. In alkyl amines, a combination of electron releasing, steric and H-bonding factors influence the stability of the substituted ammonium cations in protic polar solvents and thus affect the basic nature of amines. Alkyl amines are found to be stronger bases than ammonia. Amines being basic in nature, react with acids to form salts. Aryldiazonium salts, undergo replacement of the diazonium group with a variety of nucleophiles to produce aryl halides, cyanides, phenols and arenes.
Answer the following questions:

  1. How can you convert the following? (2)
    1. Ethanoic acid to methanamine
    2. Propanenitrile to 1-aminopropane
  2. Why is {tex}{pK}_{{b}}{/tex} value of aniline more than that of methylamine? (1)
    1. Arrange the following in increasing order of their basic strength in aqueous solution: (1){tex}{CH}_{3}-{NH}_{2}, \left({CH}_{3}\right)_{2} {NH}, \left({CH}_{3}\right)_{3} {~N}{/tex}

      OR

    2. Give the structures of A and B in the following reaction: (1)
      {tex}{C}_{6} {H}_{5} {NO}_{2} \xrightarrow{{Fe} / {HCl}} {~A} \xrightarrow[273 {~K}]{{HNO}_{2}} {~B}{/tex}
 4
30.

The Crystal Field Theory (CFT) of coordination compounds is based on the effect of different crystal fields (provided by the ligands taken as point charges) on the degeneracy of d-orbital energies of the central metal atom/ion. The splitting of the d-orbitals provides different electronic arrangements in strong and weak crystal fields. In tetrahedral coordination entity formation, the d-orbital splitting is smaller as compared to the octahedral entity.
Answer the following questions:

  1. On the basis of CFT, explain why {tex}\left[{Ti}\left({H}_{2} {O}\right)_{6}\right] {Cl}_{3}{/tex} complex is coloured? What happens on heating the complex {tex}\left[{Ti}\left({H}_{2} {O}\right)_{6}\right] {Cl}_{3}{/tex}? Give reason. (2)
    [Atomic no. : {tex}{Ti}=22{/tex}]
    1. What is crystal field splitting energy? (1)

      OR

    2. On the basis of {tex}\Delta_{0}{/tex} and P (pairing energy), how can you differentiate between a strong field ligand and a weak field ligand? (1)
  3. Why are low spin tetrahedral complexes rarely observed? (1)
 4

Section - E
31.(i)
  1. Calculate the emf of the following cell at {tex}25^{\circ} {C}{/tex}:
    {tex}{Zn}({s})\left|{Zn}^{2+}(0.1 {M}) \| {H}^{+}(0.01 {M})\right| {H}_{2}({~g})(1{/tex} bar{tex}), {Pt}({s}){/tex}
    [Given : {tex}{E}_{{Zn}^{2+} / {Zn}}^{\circ}=-0.76 {~V}, {E}_{2 {H}^{+} / {H}_{2}}^{\circ}=0.00 {~V}, \log 10=1{/tex}]
  2. State Faraday's second law of electrolysis. How much electricity is required in terms of Faraday for the reduction of 1 mol of {tex}{Cr}_{2} {O}_{7}{ }^{2-}{/tex} to {tex}{Cr}^{3+}{/tex}?
 5

OR
(ii)

Answer the following questions:

  1. The conductivity of 0.20 M solution of KCl is {tex}2.48 \times 10^{-2} {~S} {~cm}^{-1}{/tex}. Calculate its molar conductivity and degree of dissociation ({tex}\alpha{/tex}).
    {tex}{\left[\text { Given : } \lambda_{\left({K}^{+}\right)}^{\circ}\right.} =73.5 {~S} {~cm}^{2} {~mol}^{-1}{/tex}
    {tex}\lambda_{\left({Cl}^{-}\right)}^{\circ} \left.=76.5 {~S} {~cm}^{2} {~mol}^{-1}\right]{/tex}
  2. Calculate {tex}\Delta_{{r}} {G}^{\circ}{/tex} of the following cell:
    {tex}{Mg}({~s})+{Cu}^{2+}({aq}) \longrightarrow {Mg}^{2+}({aq})+{Cu}({~s}){/tex}
    {tex}{\left[\text { Given }: {E}_{{Mg}^{2+} / {Mg}}^{\circ}=-2 \cdot 37 {~V}, {E}_{{Cu}^{2+} / {Cu}}^{\circ}=+0 \cdot 34 {~V}\right.}{/tex}{tex}\left. 1 {~F}=96500 {C} {~mol}^{-1}\right]{/tex}
  3. What type of cell is mercury cell ? Why is it more advantageous than dry cell?
 5
32.(i)
  1. Account for the following:
    1. The {tex}{E}_{{Mn}^{2+} / {Mn}}^{\circ}{/tex} value for manganese is highly negative, whereas {tex}{E}_{{Mn}^{3+} / {Mn}^{2+}}^{\circ}{/tex} is highly positive.
    2. Actinoids show wide range of oxidation states.
    3. Transition metals have high melting points.
  2. Complete the following ionic equations:
    1. {tex} 5 {SO}_3{ }^{2-}+2 {MnO}_4{ }^{-}+6 {H}^{+} \longrightarrow {/tex}
    2. {tex} 2 {MnO}_4{ }^{-}+{H}_2 {O}+{I}^{-} \longrightarrow {/tex}
 5

OR
(ii)

Answer the following questions:

  1. Name two elements of 3d series for which the third ionisation enthalpies are quite high.
  2. Out of {tex}{KMnO}_4{/tex} and {tex}{K}_2 {MnO}_4{/tex}, which one is paramagnetic and why?
  3. Write any one consequence of lanthanoid contraction.
  4. How do you prepare potassium manganate from pyrolusite ore?
  5. Why is the ability of oxygen more than fluorine to stabilise higher oxidation states of transition metals?
 5
33.(i)
  1. An organic compound (X) having molecular formula {tex}{C}_5 {H}_{10} {O}{/tex} can show various properties depending on its structures.
    Draw each of the structures if it:
    1. shows Cannizzaro reaction.
    2. reduces Tollens' reagent and has a chiral carbon.
    3. gives positive iodoform test.
  2. Write the reaction involved in the following:
    1. Clemmensen reduction
    2. Etard reaction
 5

OR
(ii)

Answer the following questions :

  1. Draw structure of the methyl hemiacetal of methanal.
  2. There are two {tex}-{NH}_2{/tex} groups in semicarbazide. However only one is involved in the formation of semicarbazones. Give reason.
  3. How will you convert ethanol to 3-hydroxybutanal?
  4. Complete the following equation:
  5. Write the final product formed when phthalic acid is treated with {tex}{NH}_{3}{/tex} followed by strong heating.
 5

Explain why electrolysis of aqueous solution of NaCl gives H2 at cathode and Cl2 at anode. Write overall reaction.
{tex}\left( \begin{gathered} E_{N{a^ + }/Na}^0 = - 2.71V;\;E_{{H_2}O/{H_2}}^0 = - 0.83V \hfill \\ E_{C{l_2}/2C{l^ - }}^0 = + 1.36V;\;E_{{H^ + } + {O_2}/{H_2}O}^0 = 1.23V \hfill \\ \end{gathered} \right){/tex}

Solution:

At cathode, the following reduction reactions can take place:
{tex}Na_{(aq)}^ + + {e^ - } \to N{a_{(s)}};E_{N{a^ + }/Na}^0 = - 2.71V{/tex}
{tex}H_{(aq)}^ + + {e^ - } \to \frac{1}{2}{H_2}(g);\;E_{{H^ + }/{H_2}}^0 = OV{/tex}
A reduction reaction with higher reduction potential is preferred. Therefore, the reaction at the cathode during electrolysis is
{tex}H_{(aq)}^ + + {e^ - } \to \frac{1}{2}{H_{2(g)}}{/tex}
This is why electrolysis of aqueous solution of NaCl gives H2 at the cathode.
At anode, the following oxidation reactions can take place:
{tex}Cl_{(aq)}^ - \to \frac{1}{2}C{l_{2(g)}} + {e^ - }E_{C{l_2}/2C{l^ - }}^0 = + 1.36V{/tex}
{tex}2{H_2}{O_{(l)}} \to {O_{2(g)}} + 4H_{(aq)}^ + + 4{e^ - }E_{{H^ + } + {O_2}/{H_2}O}^0 {/tex} = 1.23V
At anode, the reaction with lower value of E0 is preferred. But, due to overvoltage, oxidation of chloride ion occurs and chlorine gas is obtained. Hence, the reaction at the anode during electrolysis is
{tex}Cl_{(aq)}^ - \to \frac{1}{2}C{l_{2(g)}} + {e^ - }\;E_{C{l_2}/2C{l^ - }}^0 = + 1.36V{/tex}
This is why electrolysis of aqueous solution of NaCl gives Cl2 at the anode.
The overall cell reaction is given below.
{tex}{H^ + } + C{l^ - } \to \frac{1}{2}{H_2} + \frac{1}{2}C{l_2}{/tex}
